title: The woman who feeds the cats
summary: A tourist asks an older woman in Istanbul why she feeds the street cats. She does not answer the way the tourist expects.

body.speaker: Mariam
body.text: Excuse me. Is this your cat?

body.speaker: Olga
body.text: No. None of them are mine. They are the city's cats.

body.speaker: Mariam
body.text: But you are feeding them. There are four bowls.

body.speaker: Olga
body.text: Five. The fifth bowl is hidden behind the wall. The grey cat is shy.

body.speaker: Mariam
body.text: Do you feed them every day?

body.speaker: Olga
body.text: Every morning and every evening. For nineteen years.

body.speaker: Mariam
body.text: That is a lot of food. Is it expensive?

body.speaker: Olga
body.text: Yes. I do not eat meat anymore. The cats eat it. We share the family budget.

body.speaker: Olga
body.text: My husband died eleven years ago. After he died, the cats came more. I think they could feel the house was emptier.

body.speaker: Olga
body.text: Do not be. They were already coming when he was alive. He used to laugh and say, 'They are our second children.' We have no first children, so the joke was complicated.

body.speaker: Mariam
body.text: Why do you keep going? You are seventy now, yes?

body.speaker: Olga
body.text: Seventy-two. I keep going because they keep coming. It is not a decision anymore. It is a small contract between me and this street.

body.speaker: Olga
body.text: The young woman across the street has been watching me for a year. She does not know I notice. Last month she bought her own bowl. We have not spoken. We do not need to.

annotations.meaningVi: mèo của thành phố (mèo hoang sống ở đường phố Istanbul)
đã đồng ý chuyện này (past simple của 'agree')
sau khi anh ấy mất — after + mệnh đề chỉ thời gian
thường hay cười (chỉ thói quen trong quá khứ)
thoả ước nhỏ / giao ước nhỏ
không có con ruột / không có đứa con đầu tiên
đã theo dõi / quan sát tôi (present perfect continuous)

grammarNotes.title: 'Used to' for past habits
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Used to + động từ nguyên thể' diễn tả thói quen hoặc trạng thái trong quá khứ mà nay không còn nữa. Trong bài: 'He used to laugh and say, "They are our second children."' — chồng cô đã có thói quen cười và nói câu đó khi còn sống, nay không còn nữa. Cấu trúc: subject + used to + base verb. Khác với 'be used to + V-ing' (đã quen với việc làm gì).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Used to + base verb' describes a past habit or state that no longer happens. From the dialogue: 'He used to laugh and say, "They are our second children."' — when he was alive, this was a regular habit; now it is gone. Form: subject + used to + base verb. Do not confuse with 'be used to + V-ing' (to be accustomed to doing something).

translationVi:
Mariam: Xin lỗi bà. Đây có phải mèo của bà không ạ?
Olga: Không. Không con nào là của tôi. Chúng là mèo của thành phố.
Mariam: Nhưng bà đang cho chúng ăn. Có bốn cái bát.
Olga: Năm. Cái thứ năm giấu sau tường. Con mèo xám nhút nhát.
Mariam: Bà cho ăn mỗi ngày à?
Olga: Mỗi sáng và mỗi tối. Suốt mười chín năm.
Mariam: Vậy nhiều thức ăn lắm. Có đắt không ạ?
Olga: Có. Tôi không ăn thịt nữa. Mèo ăn. Chúng tôi chia ngân sách gia đình.
Mariam: Gia đình bà đồng ý sao?
Olga: Chồng tôi mất mười một năm trước. Sau khi anh ấy mất, mèo đến nhiều hơn. Tôi nghĩ chúng cảm được nhà trống vắng hơn.
Mariam: Cháu xin lỗi.
Olga: Đừng. Chúng đã đến từ khi anh còn sống. Anh ấy thường cười và nói: 'Chúng là con thứ hai của chúng ta.' Vợ chồng tôi không có con đầu, nên câu đùa hơi phức tạp.
Mariam: Sao bà vẫn tiếp tục? Bà bảy mươi rồi phải không ạ?
Olga: Bảy mươi hai. Tôi tiếp tục vì chúng vẫn đến. Đây không còn là một quyết định nữa. Đây là một giao ước nhỏ giữa tôi và con phố này.
Mariam: Vậy sau này thì sao?
Olga: Cô gái trẻ bên kia đường đã quan sát tôi một năm rồi. Cô ấy không biết tôi để ý. Tháng trước cô mua bát riêng. Chúng tôi chưa nói chuyện. Chúng tôi không cần.

questions.prompt: Whose cats does Olga say they are?
Why is the fifth bowl hidden?
How long has Olga been feeding the cats?
How does she pay for the food?
options: She does not eat meat anymore; the cats eat it; they share the family budget

questions.prompt: What did the husband used to say about the cats?

options: Because she and her husband had no first children
Because the cats were too many
Because the cats had no names
questions.explanation: 'We have no first children, so the joke was complicated.'

questions.prompt: Why does Olga keep feeding the cats?
options: Because they keep coming — it is not a decision but a small contract

questions.prompt: What has the young woman across the street done?
options: Watched Olga for a year and last month bought her own bowl
A long, small kindness can be inherited without a word, simply by being watched.
All Istanbul cats belong to one family.
Older people should retire from caring.
questions.hint: The ending hands the role to a stranger.

cloze.template:
Mariam: Excuse me. Is this your cat?
Olga: No. None of them are {{b1}}. They are the city's cats.
Mariam: But you are feeding them. There are four bowls.
Olga: Five. The fifth bowl is hidden behind the wall. The grey cat is {{b2}}.
Mariam: Do you feed them every day?
Olga: Every morning and every evening. For {{b3}} years.
Mariam: That is a lot of food. Is it expensive?
Olga: Yes. I do not eat meat anymore. The cats eat it. We share the family {{b4}}.
Mariam: Your family agreed to this?
Olga: My husband {{b5}} eleven years ago. After he died, the cats came more. I think they could feel the house was {{b6}}.
Mariam: I am sorry.
Olga: Do not be. They were already coming when he was alive. He {{b7}} to laugh and say, 'They are our second children.' We have no first children, so the joke was complicated.
Mariam: Why do you keep going? You are seventy now, yes?
Olga: Seventy-two. I keep going because they keep coming. It is not a decision anymore. It is a small {{b8}} between me and this street.
Mariam: What happens after?
Olga: The young woman across the {{b9}} has been watching me for a year. She does not know I notice. Last month she bought her own bowl. We have not {{b10}}. We do not need to.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Olga says, 'It is not a decision anymore. It is a small contract between me and this street.' Is there something in your life that started as a choice but has quietly become an agreement with a place or a person? Would you sign it again knowing what it asked of you?
